plain_english_takeaway: Sucrose shock rapidly slows the flagellar motor, and larger shocks cause larger slowdowns.
TMRM fluorescence drops after shock, independently supporting membrane depolarization.
A different non-ionic osmolyte causes a similar motor slowdown, so the effect is not sucrose-specific.
The response persists without potassium in the buffer, so immediate potassium uptake is not required.
Clockwise-locked motors slow similarly, pointing upstream of motor direction switching.
During longer shock, motor speed partly recovers over minutes, consistent with adaptation and PMF restoration.
Cells shrink during osmotic shock and recover after return to base media.
Measured motor speed can be compared with a viscosity-only expectation.
